---
title: "Notes on Qwen 3.5 vs Gemma4 for Local Agentic Coding on an RTX 4090"
tags: ["LLMs", "Benchmarking", "Local LLMs", "Agentic Coding", "llama.cpp", "MoE"]
description: Benchmarking notes comparing Qwen 3.5 and Gemma4 model families (both dense and MoE variants) for local agentic coding tasks on an RTX 4090, using llama-bench and single-shot prompting with Open Code.
date: 2026-04-05
permalink: /2026-04-05-benchmarking-qwen35-vs-gemma4/
---

[Gemma4](https://deepmind.google/models/gemma/gemma-4/) was relased by google on April 2nd earlier this and I wanted to see how it performs against Qwen 3.5 for local agentic coding. This post is my notes on benchmarking the two model families. I ran two types of tests:

1. **Standard llama-bench benchmarks** for raw prefill and generation speed
2. **Single-shot agentic coding tasks** using [Open Code](https://opencode.ai) to see how these models actually perform on real multi-step coding workflows

## Models

| Model | Architecture | Quant | Size on Disk | Total Params | Active Params |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | Q4_K_XL | 16.40 GiB | 26.90 B | 26.90 B |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | Q4_K_XL | 20.70 GiB | 34.66 B | ~3 B |
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | Q4_K_XL | 15.95 GiB | 25.23 B | ~4 B |
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | Q4_K_XL | 17.46 GiB | 30.70 B | 30.70 B |

- **The size on disk is without kv-cache**
- All four models were run with thinking mode enabled and quantized to **Q4_K_XL** using [Unsloth](https://unsloth.ai/) GGUFs on [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp).
- All benchmarks were run on April 3rd, 2026.
- **Hardware**: NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM). 


## Standard Benchmarks with llama-bench

llama.cpp has a `llama-bench` utility that runs standard prefill and decode benchmarks. It is a quick way to get raw throughput numbers before doing anything more involved.

This is the command I used to run the benchmarks:

```bash
./llama.cpp/llama-bench \
  -m $MODEL_PATH \
  -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -fa 1 -b 2048 -ub 512 \
  -p 512,2048,4096,8192,16384,32768,65336 \
  -n 128 -r 3 -o md
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="llama-bench flags used"}

| Flag | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `-ctk` | `q8_0` | KV cache keys quantized to 8-bit |
| `-ctv` | `q8_0` | KV cache values quantized to 8-bit |
| `-fa` | `1` | Flash Attention enabled |
| `-b` | `2048` | Batch size (max tokens processed per batch) |
| `-ub` | `512` | Micro-batch size (tokens processed per CUDA kernel call) |
| `-p` | `512,2048,...,65336` | Prefill token counts to sweep |
| `-n` | `128` | Decode (generation) tokens per run |
| `-r` | `3` | Repeat each test 3 times and report mean |
| `-o` | `md` | Output as markdown table |

:::

### Prefill Speed

| Context | Qwen3.5-27B | Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | Gemma4-26B-A4B | Gemma4-31B |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 512 | 3,037 | 6,666 | **8,597** | 3,100 |
| 2K | 3,069 | 6,674 | **8,710** | 2,992 |
| 4K | 3,025 | 6,633 | **8,733** | 2,925 |
| 8K | 2,957 | 6,524 | **8,443** | 2,811 |
| 16K | 2,841 | 6,308 | **7,961** | 2,614 |
| 32K | 2,632 | 5,920 | **7,097** | 2,304 |
| 65K | 2,290 | 5,273 | **5,917** | 1,869 |

### Generation Speed (tg128)

| Model | Architecture | Generation (tokens/s) |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | **165.84** |
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | 164.38 |
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | 45.88 |
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | 44.42 |


### Notes on llama-bench Results

- As expected, the MoE models dominate both prefill and generation speed.
- Generation speed for the two MoE models is nearly identical (~165 tok/s). Same story for the two dense models (~45 tok/s). This emphasises the fact that generation is memory bandwidth bound not compute bound.


## Agentic Coding: One-Prompt Test

>The llama-bench numbers tell you how fast tokens move but they say nothing about how a model actually performs when it has to reason, make tool calls, write code and iterate on errors.

To test that, I ran a simple practical test: give the model one prompt and see if it can figure out the rest on its own. There will be no hand-holding and multi-turn guidance. The idea is to see how the model performs in such a scenario. This is not a formal benchmark. It is two prompts at different complexity levels to see how well the model handles mult-step workflows. As usually the case with most of us, I describe what I want in a single prompt and let the model do its thing. 

### Setup

I used [Open Code](https://opencode.ai) as the agentic coding frontend because I find it easier to set up with a local llama-server backend. I also configured [Context7](https://context7.com) as an skills + MCP server to let models fetch up-to-date library documentation and API docs during its run.

llama-server was configured with q8_0 KV cache and context size varied per model based on VRAM constraints to maximize generation speed (full config in [Appendix A](#appendix-a-hardware-fit-and-server-config)).

  - Speed metrics came from llama-server's `/metrics` endpoint (Prometheus counters).
  - Token usage breakdowns were estimated using the [opencode-tokenscope](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope) plugin. 

I also makde sure to restart llama-server between model runs so the counters would not carry over.


### Prompt 1: Simple (httpx + pytest)

```
use context7 to look up the httpx library docs. then write me a python script
that fetches the post from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1 and
prints the title. also write a pytest test for it, no mocks, hit the real API.
use uv run to run everything so we don't install anything in the current
environment. run the test and make sure it passes.
```

This tests the basics such as can the model call Context7 to look up docs, write a simple script and real integration test (no mocks), use `uv run` for running and deps. management and actually execute everything to verify it works.


### Prompt 2: Comprehensive (Nano Banana Image Generation + TDD)

```
use context7 to search for the latest google gemini image generation API docs.
I want you to write a python script that uses the google-genai SDK to generate
images using the gemini-3.1-flash-preview model (nano banana). use TDD
red-green methodology, write failing tests first then make them pass. do not
use any mock tests. use uv run to run everything so we don't install anything
in the current environment. test the script and if it works fine and generates
an image, then use this script to run image generation on the five prompts
given in prompts.json. save the images to an images folder, make sure the
folder exists, if it doesn't then create it.
```

This is a slightly heavier multi-step workflow. The model has to:

- Look up the Gemini image generation API docs via Context7
- Write a Python script using the google-genai SDK
- Follow TDD red-green methodology (write failing tests first, then make them pass)
- Use real API calls, no mocks
- Use `uv run` for dependencies
- Read `prompts.json`, generate images for all five prompts
- Handle file I/O (create output directory, save images)

The idea is to see how well the model handles and executes the mult-step workflows correctly.


### Results: Gemma4-26B-A4B

**VRAM:** ~21 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 256K tokens

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 4,338 | 4,560 |
| Generation tok/s | 135.5 | 134.4 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 17,847 | 23,204 |
| Total tokens generated | 1,623 | 3,435 |
| Prompt processing time | 4.11s | 5.09s |
| Generation time | 11.98s | 125.56s |
| API calls | 10 | 13 |
| Tool calls | 7 | 11 |
| Correct on turn | 1st | 3rd |

> **API calls** is the number of api calls opencode makes to the llm model.

**Observations:**

- It is fast with 135 tok/s generation and 4.3K+ prefill is the fastest of the four by a wide margin.
- It is also the most concise model by far with only 1,623 tokens generated on Prompt 1 and 3,435 on Prompt 2.
- **Needed 3 attempts on Prompt 2.** Despite being the fastest and most concise, it struggled with the multi-step instructions. 


### Results: Gemma4-31B

**VRAM:** ~24 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 65K tokens

> Note: The 31B dense model maxes out VRAM on a single 4090. I had to drop the context size to 65K to maintain reasonable generation speed. At 256K, generation speed degrades progressively with best speed only achievable around 65K tokens.

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 1,466 | 1,357 |
| Generation tok/s | 37.7 | 35.2 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 16,618 | 25,070 |
| Total tokens generated | 2,903 | 5,968 |
| Prompt processing time | 11.34s | 18.48s |
| Generation time | 77.07s | 169.53s |
| API calls | 10 | 16 |
| Tool calls | 8 | 14 |
| Correct on turn | 1st | 1st |


**Observations:**

- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the first turn.** This is the key result. The dense model reliability on the complex task was noticeably better.
- The prrefill at 1,466 tok/s is 3x slower than Gemma4-26B-A4B. Generation at 37.7 tok/s is 3.6x slower. You can feel the difference.
- The generated nearly twice the tokens as the MoE variant (2,903 vs 1,623 on Prompt 1), including 1,548 reasoning tokens. Heavy thinker
- Context limited to 65K is a real practical limitation, not sure whether this degradation in speed at higher context will be solved in future.


### Results: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B

**VRAM:** ~23 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 200K tokens

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 3,179 | 3,056 |
| Generation tok/s | 136.7 | 132.3 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 16,145 | 92,375 |
| Total tokens generated | 7,564 | 32,904 |
| Prompt processing time | 5.08s | 30.23s |
| Generation time | 55.32s | 248.75s |
| API calls | 13 | 30 |
| Tool calls | 11 | 28 |
| Correct on turn | 1st | 2nd |


**Observations:**

- Generation speed is excellent at 136.7 tok/s, essentially identical to Gemma4-26B-A4B. 
- But this model is **extremely verbose**. 7,564 tokens on Prompt 1 and 32,904 tokens on Prompt 2.
- Prompt 2 was the most intensive run of the entire benchmark: 30 API calls with multiple tool calls too
- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the 2nd turn**, better than Gemma4-26B-A4B (3rd turn) but still not first-try.
- The 248.7s generation time on Prompt 2. This is a direct result of such large API and tool calls.

### Results: Qwen3.5-27B

**VRAM:** ~21 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 130K tokens

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 2,474 | 2,188 |
| Generation tok/s | 44.9 | 44.6 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 15,043 | 24,385 |
| Total tokens generated | 2,867 | 11,824 |
| Prompt processing time | 6.08s | 11.14s |
| Generation time | 63.91s | 265.00s |
| API calls | 9 | 18 |
| Tool calls | 7 | 14 |
| Correct on turn | 1st | 1st |


**Observations:**

- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the first turn.** Same as Gemma4-31B. 
- Most efficient session on Prompt 1: fewest API calls (9), fewest tool calls. It is clean and focused.
- Generation at 44.9 tok/s is slower than MoE but faster than Gemma4-31B (37.7). Noticeable but workable for interactive use.
- 130K context fits comfortably in VRAM. This is a practical sweet spot: enough context for long agentic sessions without maxing out the card.


## Summary Tables

### Speed Comparison

| Model | Arch | Prefill tok/s (P1) | Prefill tok/s (P2) | Gen tok/s (P1) | Gen tok/s (P2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | **4,338** | **4,560** | 135.5 | **134.4** |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | 3,179 | 3,056 | **136.7** | 132.3 |
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | 2,474 | 2,188 | 44.9 | 44.6 |
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | 1,466 | 1,357 | 37.7 | 35.2 |

### Token Efficiency

| Model | Arch | Tokens Gen (P1) | Tokens Gen (P2) | API Calls (P1) | API Calls (P2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | **1,623** | **3,435** | 10 | **13** |
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | 2,903 | 5,968 | 10 | 16 |
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | 2,867 | 11,824 | **9** | 18 |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | 7,564 | 32,904 | 13 | 30 |

### Task Completion (Prompt 2)

| Model | Arch | Correct on Turn | Tool Calls | Estimated Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | 1st | 14 | $0.041 |
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | 1st | 14 | $0.023 |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | 2nd | 28 | $0.070 |
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | 3rd | 11 | $0.027 |

### Hardware Fit (RTX 4090 24 GB)

| Model | Arch | VRAM Usage | Max Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma4-26B-A4B | MoE | ~21 GB | 256,000 |
| Qwen3.5-27B | Dense | ~21 GB | 130,672 |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B | MoE | ~23 GB | 200,000 |
| Gemma4-31B | Dense | ~24 GB | 65,336 |


## General Impressions

A few things stood out to me from this exercise.

**Dense models were more reliable on the complex task.** Both Qwen3.5-27B and Gemma4-31B got Prompt 2 right on the first turn. Both MoE models needed retries. Two data points is not a conclusion, but it is a pattern worth noting.

**MoE speed advantage is real but verbosity can eat it up.** Both MoE models hit ~135 tok/s generation vs ~40-45 tok/s for dense. But Qwen3.5-35B-A3B generated 32,904 tokens on Prompt 2, which means 248 seconds of generation even at MoE speeds. Gemma4-26B-A4B was the only model that was both fast and concise.

**Qwen3.5-27B feels like the best overall pick for agentic coding on a 4090.** Here is my reasoning:

- Reliable: got the complex task right on the first try
- 130K context is a practical sweet spot: enough for long agentic sessions, does not max out the card
- 44.9 tok/s is slower than MoE but fast enough for interactive use
- Most efficient on the simple task (fewest API calls, fewest billing tokens)
- Only uses ~21 GB VRAM, leaving headroom
- In my initial pass analyzing the generated code quality, it seems noticeably better than the other models. I am still working through this analysis and will update this section with more detail.

Gemma4-26B-A4B is the speed king and if you are doing high-volume, simpler tasks where first-try reliability matters less, it is hard to beat. But for the kind of agentic coding I do day-to-day, where I want to fire off a prompt and trust the model to get it right, Qwen3.5-27B is still my go-to.

**Caveat:** These are notes from a single benchmarking session with two prompts. I am not claiming any of this is statistically rigorous. Different prompts, different tasks, different days could give different results. Take it as one data point.


## Code Quality (WIP)

I am still analyzing the quality of the generated code across all four models. My initial impression is that Qwen3.5-27B produces noticeably cleaner and more correct code compared to the other three. I will update this section with specifics once I have finished the analysis.


## Appendix A: Hardware Fit and Server Config {#appendix-a-hardware-fit-and-server-config}

### llama-server Launch Config

Base config used for all models:

```bash
llama-server \
    --model $MODEL_PATH \
    --jinja \
    --host 100.80.101.103 \
    --port 8001 \
    --parallel 1 \
    --batch-size 2048 \
    --ubatch-size 512 \
    --cache-type-k q8_0 --cache-type-v q8_0 \
    --flash-attn on \
    --context-shift \
    --metrics
```

### Per-Model Overrides

**Gemma4 models:**

```bash
--ctx-size 256000     # MoE (26B-A4B)
--ctx-size 65336      # Dense (31B) - reduced due to VRAM constraints
--temp 1.0
--top-p 0.95
--top-k 64
--min-p 0.00
```

**Qwen 3.5 models:**

```bash
--ctx-size 200000     # MoE (35B-A3B)
--ctx-size 130672     # Dense (27B)
--temp 0.6
--top-k 20
--chat-template-file $TEMPLATES_DIR/qwen35-chat-template-corrected.jinja
--chat-template-kwargs '{"enable_thinking":true}'
```

Note: Qwen 3.5 models used a corrected Jinja chat template and different sampling parameters than Gemma4. Thinking mode was enabled explicitly for Qwen via the chat template kwargs. For Gemma4, thinking is enabled by default.


## Appendix C: Instrumentation {#appendix-c-instrumentation}

### Tokenscope

I used the [opencode-tokenscope](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope) plugin to get per-session token breakdowns. Add `"plugin": ["@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope"]` to your `opencode.json`, then create a `/tokenscope` slash command in `~/.config/opencode/command/tokenscope.md`. After each run, typing `/tokenscope` dumps a full report.

### llama-server /metrics

llama-server exposes a `/metrics` endpoint (enabled with the `--metrics` flag) that returns Prometheus-format counters. The two key gauges are:

- `llamacpp:prompt_tokens_seconds` (prefill speed)
- `llamacpp:predicted_tokens_seconds` (generation speed)

These are cumulative across the session, so I restarted llama-server between model runs to get clean numbers.

### llama.cpp Installation

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pciutils build-essential cmake curl libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev -y
git clone https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp && git pull origin master && cd ..
cmake llama.cpp -B llama.cpp/build \
    -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF \
    -DGGML_CUDA=ON \
    -DLLAMA_CURL=ON
cmake --build llama.cpp/build --config Release -j24 --clean-first \
    --target llama-cli llama-mtmd-cli llama-server llama-gguf-split llama-bench
cp llama.cpp/build/bin/llama-* llama.cpp
```


## Appendix D: Gotchas {#appendix-d-gotchas}

### Qwen3.5-35B-A3B todowrite Parse Error

Qwen3.5-35B-A3B sometimes returned tool call arguments as a raw JSON string instead of a parsed object. This caused the `todowrite` tool to fail because Open Code expected `todos` to be an array, not a string containing an array.

Fix: a small plugin at `~/.opencode/plugins/todo-fix-plugins.ts`:

```typescript
export const TodoFixPlugin = async (ctx) => {
  return {
    "tool.execute.before": async (input, output) => {
      if (input.tool === "todowrite" && typeof output.args.todos === "string") {
        output.args.todos = JSON.parse(output.args.todos)
      }
    }
  }
}
```

This is a common issue with local models that do not always serialize tool arguments correctly. Worth keeping around for any GGUF model that struggles with structured tool outputs.

### Gemma4-31B Context Size

The 31B dense model does not fit a 256K context on a single 4090. I had to reduce to 65,336 tokens to maintain ~40 tok/s generation. You can push it higher but generation speed degrades as context grows because the dense model activates all 31B params on every forward pass and the KV-cache eats into VRAM.

### Sampling Parameter Differences

The two model families use different recommended sampling parameters. Gemma4 uses temp 1.0 with top-k 64, while Qwen 3.5 uses temp 0.6 with top-k 20. I kept each family at its recommended settings rather than forcing the same params across all four, since the goal was to see how each model performs under its intended conditions.

### Qwen3.5 Corrected Jinja Template

Qwen3.5 models needed a corrected Jinja chat template (`qwen35-chat-template-corrected.jinja`) to work properly with llama-server. The default template had issues with thinking mode. If you are running these models, check whether your template handles the thinking tags correctly.
